text:
I gave a talk at strangeloop on a clojure library called meander which is pretty heavily inspired by term rewriting. At the end of my talk I talk about some things this sort of approach could enable in the future.
<https://github.com/noprompt/meander/>

text:
That looks very cool. The skeptical part of me sees a 4 minute demo and is reminded of 3D editor demos in the early 90s. A representive from Alias or Wavefront or one of the other early 90s 3D software companies and they'd build an entire notre-dame cathedral in 4 minutes or less. They'd start with box, extrude, subdivide, extrude, morph, duplicate, etc and in just a few operations they'd have a highly detailed 3D cathedral.

But, of you go watch 3D artists today make the same thing it takes days or weeks. Nothing has changed. The software still has all those same operations that were demoed 25 years ago. It's just something about the actual process is not fast. Probably the difference between first implementation and 2nd. Knowing exactly what I want and having done it before I can take the 5 steps to get there. But not having done it before and not knowing exactly what I want there will be 150 steps.

I guess that makes me wonder what it's like to use this sofware when I'm exploring. Will I be fighting it all the time because some invisible constraint is preventing me from making the edit I want and then I have to go spend time figuring out where that constraint is so I can remove it?

A representive from Alias or Wavefront or one of the other early 90s 3D software companies and they'd build an entire notre-dame cathedral in 4 minutes or less. They'd start with box, extrude, subdivide, extrude, morph, duplicate, etc and in just a few operations they'd have a highly detailed 3D cathedral.

But, of you go watch 3D artists today make the same thing it takes days or weeks. Nothing has changed. The software still has all those same operations that were demoed 25 years ago. It's just something about the actual process is not fast. Probably the difference between first implementation and 2nd. Knowing exactly what I want and having done it before I can take the 5 steps to get there. But not having done it before and not knowing exactly what I want there will be 150 steps.

I guess that makes me wonder what it's like to use this sofware when I'm exploring. Will I be fighting it all the time because some invisible constraint is preventing me from making the edit I want and then I have to go spend time figuring out where that constraint is so I can remove it?

text: I really like the idea of a open shared interlinked knowledge space. But I'm not sure (anymore?) wiki's serve this prupose well. Since last year I'm starting to get into the zettelkasten method.  One aspect which is important with zettelkasten is the relations between the zettel. I guess Luhman kept a kind of representation of the numbers - which do represent the relations - in his head

text:
To watch people use a product with a low overhead there is also a concept called “Guerrilla UX/usability testing”,
<https://www.uxbooth.com/articles/the-art-of-guerrilla-usability-testing/>

See also “The User Experience Team of One: A Research and Design Survival Guide”
<https://www.amazon.co.uk/User-Experience-Team-One-Research/dp/1933820187/ref=cm_cr_arp_d_pdt_img_top?ie=UTF8>

And in some cases, services like <http://usertesting.com|usertesting.com> can be useful, too:
<https://www.usertesting.com>

text: Glad you liked the talk! <@UBN9AFS0N>. <@UJBAJNFLK> already mentioned some, Pure, Mathematica and Maude. I'd add to that list Stratego, and Txl for influences on meander. But the biggest influence on me is actually "PILOT: A Step Toward Man-Computer Symbiosis" by Warren Teitelman. I first encountered it in this essay by Minsky <https://web.media.mit.edu/~minsky/papers/Why%20programming%20is--.html>

text:
<http://hyperknowledge.org/>
>  We want to establish a Web of Knowledge. This allows to see how any idea is connected with other ideas in debates, through evidence, citations, influence, consequences, etc.
> There have been, and still are many attempts to create such a Web of Knowledge, and rather than be one more such attempt on our own, we want to help unify the forces of knowledge workers, both individuals and organizations, into an interoperable mesh of knowledge where everyone can contribute. We also want to make it practical to develop knowledge applications that consume and expose knowledge on this mesh.
> The protocol supports interoperability of knowledge at personal and organisational levels and federation of knowledge sources so that knowledge can be synthesised to augment our ability to learn, understand, to be understood, solve problems and achieve goals.

text: Hi folks! Recent lurker. I’m a Senior Engineer at Calendly by day, standard Ruby/React stuff. By night, I construct NES emulators in Common Lisp and ReasonML. I think Paul Chiusano’s Unison and <@UCUSW7WVD>’s Mu are two extremely interesting  examples of FutureComputing :tm: but coming from totally different directions. I wrote up some of my Research Goals here but never made it to grad school so there’s hasn’t been a ton of progress :confused: : <https://blog.kingcons.io/posts/Research-Goals.html>

text:
Having done grad school myself, I think it's overrated how realistic it is for research. I certainly benefited from it, don't get me wrong. But from a purely research perspective it's not clear to me that staying in academia is the better option for everyone who wants to do research. Particularly if the things you want to study don't have established 'grooves' already carved out. All the most interesting subjects of study, in other words :smile:

Ignoring selfish considerations, the ideal route may be 4 years in grad school, then dropping out. That should give you all the benefits (free time, access to intellectual breadth, afternoon seminars, library) and minimize the drawbacks (pressure to publish, pressure to fundraise, pressure to conform to established research paths, darwinian selection for tenure-track positions).

text:
I definitely hear you on “most interesting subjects of study lack grooves”. I think part of my problem is I have _seventy_ Programming or CompSci books at home that I’m _not_ getting through (everything from Category Theory for Programmers to Concepts, Techniques and Models to Computation Structures).

I view it mostly as a way to buy time so, yeah, 4 years then dropping out sounds positively _ace_! Thanks Kartik.
